concept: Language / The Whole of a People's Speech
description: A language: the whole system of a people's speech — its words, sounds, and ways. It is built from speech's breath and flow, grounded by 'ma': speaking made a shared whole. Not the tongue: 'lathoe' is anatomy only, and Phi does not copy English's body-metaphor for speech. Phi itself is a haluma, and this word is how the language names its own kind.
sound_symbolism: 'Ha' begins in open breath, the material of speech. 'Lu' lets that breath flow between people, and 'ma' grounds it as a durable shared system. The form remains kin to 'haolu' (speak) without containing the verb whole.
grammatical_notes: Noun. A counted language may take optional 'nophe': 'ta nophe haluma' (one language); 'ta haluma' is also grammatical. 'mia wi haluma sano.' (I know two languages.) From the Babel text: 'theula muila ta haluma to phelu.' (The whole earth held one language.)

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The field's own object, named at last: a language as a practice a people shares, not an instrument one wields. That the word grounds speech in 'ma' is the claim in miniature.

tags.community: What a people holds in common
